examples.title: TCP Checksum Calculation Algorithm
examples.description: Pseudo-code to calculate TCP checksum using pseudo-header
examples.code:
BEGIN
 // Construct pseudo-header
 pseudoHeader.srcIP = sourceIPAddress
 pseudoHeader.destIP = destIPAddress
 pseudoHeader.zero = 0
 pseudoHeader.protocol = 6 // TCP protocol number
 pseudoHeader.tcpLength = tcpHeaderLength + dataLength

 // Initialize checksum to 0
 checksum = 0

 // Sum all 16-bit words in pseudo-header
 FOR each 16-bit word in pseudoHeader:
 checksum = checksum + word

 // Sum all 16-bit words in TCP header (exclude checksum field)
 FOR each 16-bit word in tcpHeader (excluding checksum field):
 checksum = checksum + word

 // Sum all 16-bit words in data
 FOR each 16-bit word in data:
 checksum = checksum + word

 // Fold 32-bit sum to 16 bits (add carry bits to LSB)
 WHILE checksum > 0xFFFF:
 checksum = (checksum AND 0xFFFF) + (checksum >> 16)

 // One's complement
 checksum = NOT checksum

 RETURN checksum // 16-bit result
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm computes the TCP checksum at sender. At receiver, same computation including stored checksum should yield 0xFFFF (all bits 1) for valid segment. The pseudo-header ensures protection against IP-level errors.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n = total bytes (header + data)

examples.title: TCP Segment Receiver Processing
examples.description: Pseudo-code showing how receiver processes incoming TCP segment
examples.code:
BEGIN
 FUNCTION receiveSegment(segment):
 // Step 1: Validate basic header
 IF segment.dataOffset < 5 THEN
 RETURN ERROR // Invalid: header too short
 END IF

 // Step 2: Verify checksum (if checksum enabled)
 calculatedChecksum = calculateChecksum(segment)
 IF calculatedChecksum != 0x0000 THEN
 DROP segment // Silent drop - no notification
 RETURN
 END IF

 // Step 3: Check if segment is within receive window
 IF segment.seqNumber < receiveNext OR
 segment.seqNumber > (receiveNext + windowSize - 1) THEN
 // Out of window - may be old or future data
 IF segment.seqNumber < receiveNext THEN
 // Old segment - send ACK for current state
 sendACK(receiveNext)
 END IF
 DROP segment
 RETURN
 END IF

 // Step 4: Process based on flags
 IF segment.RST THEN
 CLOSE connection
 RETURN
 END IF

 IF segment.SYN THEN
 // SYN attack protection or new connection?
 RESET connection
 RETURN
 END IF

 // Step 5: Buffer data and send ACK
 bufferData(segment)
 sendACK(calculateNextAck(segment))

 IF segment.FIN THEN
 signalEndOfStream()
 END IF
 END FUNCTION
END
examples.explanation: Receiver processes segments by: validation, checksum verification, window check, flag handling, buffering data, and sending acknowledgments. Out-of-window segments are dropped or ACKed appropriately.
examples.timeComplexity: O(k) where k = bytes to buffer

examples.title: TCP Sliding Window with Segment Transmission
examples.description: Pseudo-code demonstrating sliding window protocol with segment transmission
examples.code:
BEGIN
 // TCP Sender Variables
 sendBase = 0 // Oldest unacknowledged byte
 nextSeqNum = 0 // Next sequence number to use
 windowSize = 1000 // Advertised receiver window
 MSS = 536 // Maximum Segment Size

 FUNCTION sendData(dataSize):
 // Check if window allows more transmission
 WHILE (nextSeqNum < sendBase + windowSize) AND
 (dataRemaining > 0):
 
 segmentSize = MIN(MSS, dataRemaining)
 
 // Create segment
 segment.seqNumber = nextSeqNum
 segment.data = getDataFromBuffer(nextSeqNum, segmentSize)
 segment.ackNumber = 0
 segment.ACK = FALSE
 
 // Send segment
 sendToNetwork(segment)
 
 // Record in sent but not ACKed
 markSent(segment.seqNumber, segmentSize)
 
 nextSeqNum = nextSeqNum + segmentSize
 dataRemaining = dataRemaining - segmentSize
 END WHILE
 END FUNCTION

 FUNCTION handleAck(ackNumber):
 IF ackNumber > sendBase THEN
 // Update send base - slide window
 removeAcknowledgedData(sendBase, ackNumber)
 sendBase = ackNumber
 
 // If window opened, continue sending
 IF windowWasZero AND (windowSize > 0) THEN
 signal to resume transmission
 END IF
 END IF
 END FUNCTION
END
examples.explanation: This demonstrates TCP sliding window: sender maintains 'sendBase' (oldest unacked) and 'nextSeqNum'. Can send up to windowSize bytes ahead. ACKs advance sendBase, opening the window for new transmissions.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) per segment transmission, O(n) for ACK processing
